Title: Child & adolescent anxiety and family accommodation : exploring the role of cognitive coping
Effect Size / Interaction Term: Passive exposure β=.18; feedback monitoring β=.14; problematic use β=.29; small moderation effects involving stress and sleep quality.
Key Finding: More passive/problematic social-media exposure was linked to higher anxiety, while active engagement was weaker.
Limitation: Cross-sectional; no family accommodation × digital exposure term.
Summary: Adolescents’ passive social-media exposure, feedback monitoring, and problematic use were associated with higher anxiety in a cross-sectional sample. The study also reported small moderation effects involving stress and sleep quality, but it did not test family accommodation as part of the model.

Title: Cross‐sectional and longitudinal associations of screen time with adolescent depression and anxiety
Authors: Sophie H Li, P. Batterham, Alexis E. Whitton, Kate Maston, Asaduzzaman Khan, Helen Christensen, A. Werner‐Seidler
Effect Size / Interaction Term: Cross-sectional: +1.25 depression and +0.79 anxiety per additional hour; longitudinal: +0.15 depression, no anxiety association.
Key Finding: Longitudinal associations were weak; screen time showed no later anxiety association.
Limitation: No family accommodation variable; self-reported screen time.
Summary: In the Future Proofing cohort, screen time was associated with anxiety cross-sectionally, but the longitudinal association with anxiety was null and the depression association was small. This argues against a strong direct causal effect of screen time on later anxiety in adolescents.

Title: Phenomenology and clinical correlates of family accommodation in pediatric anxiety disorders.
Authors: E. Storch, A. Salloum, C. Johnco, Brittney F. Dane, Erika A. Crawford, Morgan A. King, N. McBride, A. Lewin
Key Finding: Accommodation was present in all families and tracked more severe anxiety and impairment.
Limitation: No digital exposure measure; no interaction test.
Summary: In anxious youth, family accommodation was common and associated with greater anxiety severity, externalizing behaviors, and functional impairment. The paper did not include digital exposure measures, so it informs the accommodation side of the question only.

Title: Youth Anxiety: The Moderating Effects of Accommodation and Emotional Warmth
Authors: T. Gladstone, Emily P. Wilton, Sydney D Biscarri Clark, A. Lahoud, C. Flessner
Summary: This moderation study found that family accommodation changed the relationship between emotional warmth and youth anxiety. It is relevant as an interaction paper, but the moderator and predictor were family variables rather than digital exposure.
